summary: DHH explains why Basecamp makes cancellation effortless, with no guilt trips or dark patterns. When someone asked what data drove this 'strategy,' he argues it's not a strategy at all — it's a core value. He critiques the growth-hacking mindset that optimizes retention through hassle, pointing out that the easy-to-measure savings from preventing cancellations mask the harder-to-measure reputational damage inflicted on the vast majority of customers. Ultimately, he argues that some business decisions should be driven by principles, not metrics.
key_points: Basecamp lets customers cancel without friction, guilt, or retention tactics — and it leaves a positive lasting impression
The growth-hacking mindset demands data justification for every decision, but some decisions are about values, not optimization
Easy metrics create a tyranny: you can measure the $32K saved by hassling cancellers, but not the long-term reputational damage to the 99.9% who resented the experience
Even the ROI argument misses the point — running a business you're proud of means refusing to A/B test your principles
money_quotes: We don't hassle customers who want to cancel Basecamp because that simply feels like a shitty thing to do.
Few things in the world of business aggrieves me as much as being held hostage to a subscription I no longer desire to keep.
It always leaves an incredibly sour taste, ruins any residual affection I might have had for the service, and dials in a determination to spread that ill will far and wide.
See! We earned an extra $32,856 last year putting everyone who wanted to cancel through the wringer. Yes, but at what cost?
This is the tyranny of easy metrics.
It's easy to measure how much money is saved by preventing cancelations, it's much harder to measure how much long-term business is lost by poisoning your reputation.
This isn't just about the money, it's about running a business in a way you can feel proud about.
And the only way to do that consistently is by not A/B testing your core values.
key_insight: Some business decisions should be driven by principles rather than metrics, because the tyranny of easy measurement leads companies to optimize for small, quantifiable gains while ignoring the massive, unquantifiable costs of treating customers poorly.
